text: The lesion at the head of the pancreas is visible. The surgeon uses a ligasure device to extensively cocharize the duodenum, reaching the ligament of Treitz from the right side. The right gastroepiploic vein is dissected, clipped, and divided, allowing access to the retropancreatic window with a clearly identified plane.

The surgeon cross staples the antrum with two firings of the laparoscopic linear stapler. The common hepatic artery is identified, and the gastroduodenal artery is dissected using hook cautery, then clipped and divided. The bile duct is dissected away from the portal vein and divided using cold scissors. The neck of the pancreas is transected using hook cautery, with the observation that the pancreas is very soft, making duct identification challenging.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and transects the jejunum 15 centimeters distal to it using a laparoscopic linear stapler. The mesentery of the proximal jejunum is divided with a ligature device up to the ligament of Treitz, which is completely taken down. The jejunum is then repositioned to the right infrahepatic area.
The surgeon returns to the retropancreatic window, where the lesion at the head of the pancreas is completely dissected away from the superior mesenteric vein using the ligasure device.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a retrieval bag and extracts it through a suprapubic skin incision. The frozen section analysis confirms negative margins.

The surgeon creates an opening in the mesocolon using blunt dissection. The stapled end of the jejunum is transferred through this opening into the supracolic compartment. A one-centimeter opening is made in the jejunum using the harmonic scalpel. The hepatico-jejunostomy is then performed with a single layer of interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures, using intracorporeal knots.

Due to the soft texture of the pancreas and unclear visualization of the duct, the surgeon performs a dunking technique. A posterior interrupted layer of Tuo silk sutures is placed between the pancreatic capsule and the seromuscular layer of the bowel using an RB needle. An enterotomy is created with the harmonic scalpel, followed by a second posterior layer using a running suture technique with two O proline on an RB needle.

The surgeon creates the first anterior inner layer of the anastomosis using two-O proline sutures on an RB needle, taking full thickness bites of the small bowel and the cut margin of the pancreas. This is followed by the anterior outer layer using two-O silk sutures on an RB needle, stitching between the seromuscular layer of the small bowel and the pancreatic capsule. The completed anastomosis appears healthy.

The surgeon selects the jejunum approximately thirty centimeters distal to the hepaticojejunostomy for the gastrojejunostomy. Two stay sutures are placed between the seromuscular layer of the small bowel and the stomach wall. A small enterotomy and gastrotomy are created, and a common channel is constructed using a sixty millimeter laparoscopic linear stapler.
